nodes.content:
Essay thesis

Orwell argues that bad language and bad politics reinforce each other. Bad prose is not just ugly; it helps conceal and produce bad thought.

> It becomes ugly and inaccurate because our thoughts are foolish, but the slovenliness of our language makes it easier for us to have foolish thoughts.

> Political language... is designed to make lies sound truthful and murder respectable, and to give an appearance of solidity to pure wind.
nodes.source_text: Political language... is designed to make lies sound truthful and murder respectable.

nodes.content: Problem and reversibility
Vices of modern prose
How bad writing works
Politics and evasion
Remedy and rules

Fatalism about decline

Orwell rejects the idea that bad English is inevitable. Fatalism treats language like a natural growth rather than an instrument people shape.

> It is generally assumed that we cannot by conscious action do anything about it.

> Underneath this lies the half-conscious belief that language is a natural growth and not an instrument which we shape for our own purposes.
nodes.source_text: It is generally assumed that we cannot by conscious action do anything about it.

nodes.content:
The vicious circle

Thought and language form a feedback loop. Foolish thought makes language ugly; ugly language makes foolish thought easier.

> A man may take to drink because he feels himself to be a failure, and then fail all the more completely because he drinks.

> But if thought corrupts language, language can also corrupt thought.
nodes.source_text: But if thought corrupts language, language can also corrupt thought.

nodes.content:
Reversibility

Bad habits spread by imitation, but they can be avoided. Clear prose is therefore a political act, not just a literary preference.

> The process is reversible.

> To think clearly is a necessary first step toward political regeneration.
nodes.source_text: The process is reversible.

nodes.content:
Two common faults

The five specimens share stale imagery and lack of precision. Concrete thought melts into abstract prefabrication.

> The first is staleness of imagery; the other is lack of precision.

> Prose consists less and less of words chosen for the sake of their meaning.
nodes.source_text: The first is staleness of imagery; the other is lack of precision.

nodes.content:
Dying metaphors

Worn-out metaphors save writers from making fresh images. Mixed metaphors prove the writer is not seeing what the words claim to show.

> A newly invented metaphor assists thought by evoking a visual image.

> The sole aim of a metaphor is to call up a visual image.
nodes.source_text: The sole aim of a metaphor is to call up a visual image.

nodes.content:
Verbal false limbs

Operators and inflated verb phrases replace simple verbs. They pad sentences, hide agency, and make weak statements sound orderly.

> The keynote is the elimination of simple verbs.

> Instead of being a single word, such as break, stop, spoil, mend, kill, a verb becomes a phrase.
nodes.source_text: The keynote is the elimination of simple verbs.

nodes.content:
Pretentious diction

Grand, foreign, scientific, or Latinate words dress up simple claims and biased judgments.

> Bad writers... are nearly always haunted by the notion that Latin or Greek words are grander than Saxon ones.

> The result, in general, is an increase in slovenliness and vagueness.
nodes.source_text: Latin or Greek words are grander than Saxon ones.

nodes.content:
Meaningless words

Critical and political words become empty when no one expects them to point to anything definite.

> The word Fascism has now no meaning except in so far as it signifies something not desirable.

> The defenders of every kind of regime claim that it is a democracy.
nodes.source_text: The word Fascism has now no meaning except... something not desirable.

nodes.content:
Ecclesiastes parody

Orwell translates a vivid biblical sentence into abstract modern prose to show what is lost: images disappear, syllables multiply, and meaning shrinks.

> The concrete illustrations - race, battle, bread - dissolve into the vague phrase success or failure in competitive activities.

> The whole tendency of modern prose is away from concreteness.
nodes.source_text: The whole tendency of modern prose is away from concreteness.

nodes.content:
Ready-made phrases

Bad prose is easy because ready-made phrases remove the need to choose words or images.

> It consists in gumming together long strips of words which have already been set in order by someone else.

> They will construct your sentences for you - even think your thoughts for you, to a certain extent.
nodes.source_text: They will construct your sentences for you - even think your thoughts for you.

nodes.content:
The machine speaker

Political cliche turns speakers into mechanisms. Familiar phrases allow speech without fresh perception or thought.

> One almost never finds in them a fresh, vivid, home-made turn of speech.

> A speaker who uses that kind of phraseology has gone some distance toward turning himself into a machine.
nodes.source_text: Turning himself into a machine.

nodes.content:
Defending the indefensible

Political language becomes euphemism because brutal policies cannot be defended plainly without moral shock.

> Political speech and writing are largely the defence of the indefensible.

> Such phraseology is needed if one wants to name things without calling up mental pictures of them.
nodes.source_text: Political speech and writing are largely the defence of the indefensible.

nodes.content:
Euphemism as concealment

Violence is renamed: bombing becomes pacification, forced migration becomes transfer of population, and imprisonment or murder becomes elimination of unreliable elements.

> A mass of Latin words falls upon the facts like soft snow, blurring the outlines and covering up all the details.
nodes.source_text: A mass of Latin words falls upon the facts like soft snow.

nodes.content:
Insincerity is the enemy

The deepest cause of cloudy language is the gap between real and declared aims.

> The great enemy of clear language is insincerity.

> When there is a gap between one's real and one's declared aims, one turns as it were instinctively to long words and exhausted idioms.
nodes.source_text: The great enemy of clear language is insincerity.

nodes.content:
Cure by vigilance

Because bad phrases invade the mind automatically, reform starts with being constantly on guard.

> This invasion of one's mind by ready-made phrases... can only be prevented if one is constantly on guard against them.

> Every such phrase anaesthetizes a portion of one's brain.
nodes.source_text: Every such phrase anaesthetizes a portion of one's brain.

nodes.content:
What reform is not

Defending English is not archaism, standard-English policing, fake simplicity, or automatic preference for Saxon words.

> It has nothing to do with archaism.

> What is above all needed is to let the meaning choose the word, and not the other way about.
nodes.source_text: Let the meaning choose the word.

nodes.content:
Six rules

Orwell offers practical rules: avoid familiar figures, prefer short words, cut what can be cut, use active voice, avoid needless jargon, and break any rule rather than write barbarously.

> Never use a metaphor, simile or other figure of speech which you are used to seeing in print.

> If it is possible to cut a word out, always cut it out.

> Never use the passive where you can use the active.
nodes.source_text: If it is possible to cut a word out, always cut it out.

nodes.content:
Rules need attitude

The rules are elementary, but they demand a deeper change: stop surrendering to fashionable verbal habits and choose words by meaning.

> These rules sound elementary, and so they are, but they demand a deep change of attitude.

> One could keep all of them and still write bad English, but one could not write the kind of stuff that I quoted.
nodes.source_text: They demand a deep change of attitude.

nodes.content:
Final political payoff

Simplifying English does not solve politics by itself, but it prevents the worst follies of orthodoxy from speaking through you.

> You cannot speak any of the necessary dialects, and when you make a stupid remark its stupidity will be obvious, even to yourself.

> One can at least change one's own habits.
nodes.source_text: One can at least change one's own habits.
